compiled_state.description:
`rebar review-plan` (the four-pass plan-review gate) is **non-deterministic and mis-calibrated**
as a gate: the same ticket re-reviewed gives different PASS/BLOCK verdicts and a different finding
set each run, the advisory batch is a near-constant ~10 regardless of plan quality, and almost
every finding is tagged `severity=critical` while remaining non-blocking. The Pass-1 finder
recall is stochastic, so "PASS" is not reproducible for the same input — which undermines a gate
whose contract is "must PASS before you can claim." (The finding CONTENT is consistently high
quality; this bug is about stability/calibration, not correctness.)

## Reproduction Steps
1. Run `rebar review-plan <id> --output json` on a well-formed ticket; note `verdict` + the
   `blocking`/`advisory` arrays.
2. Without changing the ticket (or after a change that only ADDS detail/hardening), re-run.
3. Compare verdicts, finding counts, and which findings appear.

## Expected
- The verdict is reproducible for the same input (same ticket -> same PASS/BLOCK), and finding
  count/severity reflect actual plan quality (a strong plan yields fewer/lower-severity findings
  than a weak one).

## Actual (observed across ~29 runs this session on epic d251 + its children)
- **Non-reproducible verdicts.** S4/S6/S7 went `PASS -> BLOCK` after remediations that only added
  hardening, and each re-run surfaced DIFFERENT blocking findings; S6's block count was
  non-monotonic across rounds: `0 -> 3 -> 1 -> 6`. The same plan can pass for one run and block
  for the next.
- **Fixed-size advisory batch.** Almost every ticket, every round, returned ~10 advisories
  (`P/10a` near-uniformly; one ticket once at 8) — reads as a target/cap, not a measurement, so
  advisory volume does not discriminate plan quality.
- **Severity inflation.** Most advisories are `severity=critical` (e.g. epic 10/10, S3 10/10)
  yet non-blocking (`reason: default-advisory`) — "critical but doesn't block" is incoherent and
  makes severity useless for triage.
- **Weak cross-criterion dedup.** A single root issue surfaced as multiple findings under
  different criteria (e.g. S1's `use_lockfile`/TF>=1.10 issue appeared 4x under T1/T3/E2/G6),
  inflating the count.

## Impact
"Re-run until it passes" (the gate protocol) assumes convergence that doesn't hold: remediating
the cited block can surface new, unrelated blocks, so a good-faith author may not reach a stable
PASS. Two agents reviewing the same ticket can get different claimability.

## Notes
- Likely root: Pass-3 (decide) is deterministic GIVEN the findings, but Pass-1 (finder) recall is
  stochastic, so determinism is only conditional on a non-deterministic input. The fixed-~10 and
  all-critical patterns suggest finder prompt/threshold calibration. Surfaced dogfooding d251.
- Separate, more clear-cut bug filed for signed-on-BLOCK.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Re-running `review-plan` on an unchanged ticket yields a stable verdict (PASS stays PASS;
      no spurious PASS<->BLOCK flips) — e.g. via seeding/caching the finder, lowered temperature,
      or a deterministic finder pass, measured over N repeat runs.
- [ ] Advisory count reflects actual issues found (not a near-constant ~10); document/justify any
      cap and surface when findings were truncated.
- [ ] Severity is calibrated so `critical` implies blocking (or the block thresholds are aligned
      so a wall of "critical" advisories cannot coexist with a clean PASS); severity becomes
      usable for triage.
- [ ] Cross-criterion dedup collapses one root issue reported under multiple criteria into a
      single finding.
- [ ] docs/plan-review-gate.md documents the determinism guarantees and the calibration of
      severity vs. blocking thresholds.
compiled_state.title: plan-review: non-deterministic verdict + mis-calibrated findings (fixed ~10 advisories, all 'critical', PASS<->BLOCK flips)
